nativeRange.habitat: Coastal river systems and streams of southeastern Brazil; occurs in slower-moving sections of rivers, lakes, and reservoirs with soft sandy or silty substrates and variable vegetation; tolerates a wide temperature range from cool highland streams to warmer lowland waters

decorPreferences: deep fine sand substrate (minimum 5 cm) for sand-sifting behaviour
smooth rounded stones and slate for territory delineation
driftwood roots and branches for cover
robust or potted plants (Echinodorus, Vallisneria) — sand-sifting can uproot fine-rooted plants
open sandy foraging areas in the foreground

diet.notes: Classic sand-sifter: takes mouthfuls of substrate, extracts invertebrates, detritus, and organic matter, then expels the sand through the gills. In the aquarium accepts high-quality cichlid pellets, sinking wafers, frozen and live foods (bloodworm, earthworm, brine shrimp, daphnia), and blanched vegetables. Avoid excessive protein-only diets; a varied diet with some vegetable matter mirrors the wild omnivorous intake. Feed sinking foods to prevent surface competition.

goodWith: other robust South American cichlids of similar size (Satanoperca, Acarichthys, Cichla only in very large tanks)
large characins (silver dollars, giant danios) as dither fish
armoured catfish (large Loricariidae such as Pterygoplichthys, Hypostomus)
avoidWith: small tetras and nano fish (risk of predation or persecution)
delicate substrate plants — uprooted by constant sand-sifting
overly aggressive large cichlids (large Cichlasoma, Amphilophus) that will bully or injure

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Geophagus brasiliensis — adult male showing iridescent pearl-spotted flanks and red-edged finnage.

summary: The Pearl Eartheater is the type species of Geophagus and one of the few remaining after the genus was substantially revised (López-Fernández & Taphorn 2008). A robust, cool-tolerant cichlid reaching 25+ cm from coastal southeastern Brazil; a dedicated sand-sifter and larvophilous maternal mouthbrooder suited to spacious, well-filtered South American biotope tanks.
careNotes: Geophagus brasiliensis is the nominate type species of the genus and the anchor after a major taxonomic revision dispersed many former 'Geophagus' into Gymnogeophagus, Mikrogeophagus, Satanoperca, and related genera (López-Fernández & Taphorn 2008+). Wild fish range across coastal river drainages of southeastern Brazil (Rio de Janeiro south to Rio Grande do Sul), where water temperatures can drop to around 16 °C in winter — making G. brasiliensis one of the most cold-tolerant cichlids in the hobby and a viable candidate for unheated or lightly heated tanks in temperate climates, provided the lower range is not sustained for extended periods. Adults commonly reach 22–25 cm in the aquarium (wild fish up to 28 cm); a tank of at least 300 L with a 150 cm footprint is the practical minimum for a pair. Fine sand at least 5 cm deep is non-negotiable: the species sifts substrate constantly, extracting invertebrates and organic matter, and will shift the entire bottom decoration over time. Hardy potted plants (Echinodorus spp., Vallisneria) or large stones can anchor decor. Water quality must be maintained diligently — large cichlids with messy feeding habits require robust filtration (canister filter rated at 4–6× tank volume/hour recommended) with regular partial water changes. pH of 6.5–7.5 and moderate hardness are ideal, though the species tolerates somewhat alkaline or soft water within its broad natural range. Conspecific aggression is moderate; breeding pairs become significantly more territorial and may rearrange the entire tank during spawning. A spare tank for fry raising is advisable.
breedingNotes: G. brasiliensis is a larvophilous maternal mouthbrooder — eggs are laid on a cleaned flat surface or in a pit, and the female broods hatched free-swimming larvae in her mouth (not the eggs themselves, unlike some other substrate-spawning cichlids). Spawning triggers include a slight temperature increase, large water changes, and conditioning with live or frozen foods. Clutch sizes typically range from 100 to 300+ eggs. The female broods larvae for approximately 10–14 days after hatching; during this period the male guards the territory aggressively. Fry are released as free-swimming juveniles capable of taking baby brine shrimp and micro-crushed foods immediately. The female may continue to take fry back into her mouth when threatened for a brief period post-release. Breeding in the aquarium is achievable but requires adequate space, appropriate conditioning, and ideally separating the pair from aggressive tankmates during spawning.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Geophagus brasiliensis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 28 cm TL), temperature range (16–28°C), pH (6.0–7.8), hardness, native range (SE Brazil coastal drainages), habitat, diet, sand-sifting behaviour, larvophilous mouthbrooding details, and compatibility guidance. Confirmed type-species status and genus revision context.

additional.name: FishBase — Geophagus brasiliensis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 28 cm TL), temperature (16–28°C), native range (SE Brazil, Rio Grande do Sul to Rio de Janeiro and beyond), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), and diet notes. Confirmed broad temperature tolerance and omnivorous sand-sifting diet. Lifespan estimated 8–15 years based on cichlid longevity data for species of this size class.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Eartheater Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide providing hobbyist-oriented guidance on tank size, substrate requirements (fine sand, 5+ cm depth), feeding, and compatibility. Confirmed intermediate care level, minimum tank size recommendations, and sand-sifting decor considerations.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Geophagus brasiliensis species profile
additional.notes: Provided supplementary information on conspecific aggression, breeding behaviour (larvophilous mouthbrooding), pair bonding, and temperature tolerance including cool-water hardiness. Confirmed semi-aggressive temperament and pair grouping recommendation.

additional.name: López-Fernández H & Taphorn DC (2004) — Geophaginae phylogeny and classification
additional.notes: Reference for the genus-level reclassification of former Geophagus species into Gymnogeophagus, Mikrogeophagus, Satanoperca, and allied genera. Confirms G. brasiliensis as the type species remaining in Geophagus sensu stricto. Referenced via FishBase nomenclature records.

fish.breedingNotes: Larvophilous maternal mouthbrooder; broods free-swimming larvae (not eggs) orally for ~10–14 days post-hatch. Clutches of 100–300+ eggs laid on cleaned flat surfaces or substrate pits. Breeding pair become highly territorial; space and tankmate management are key. Fry accept baby brine shrimp and micro-foods immediately upon release.
